title: Mpox Research | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
Mpox is an emerging zoonotic disease that has re-entered the global spotlight due to recent multi-country outbreaks. Once geographically confined, the current epidemiological landscape is marked by the rise of new viral clades, necessitating updated approaches to surveillance, clinical guidance, and risk communication.
Outbreaks have disproportionately affected gay, bisexual, and other men who have sex with men (GBMSM), and responses have required a balance of urgency, equity, and clarity in public health messaging. Amid this evolving context, my work aims to inform national-level responses in Canada through applied research and knowledge translation.


As part of my work with the National Collaborating Centre for Infectious Diseases, I led a project that sought to strengthen Canada’s mpox preparedness and response. This work focused on three key domains:

Critical assessment of emerging clades
Comprehensive evaluation of novel mpox clades, identifying variations in clinical presentation, disease progression, and transmissibility to inform updated case management and intervention strategies.
Epidemiological assessment of case reporting
In-depth analysis of case trends and transmission dynamics to assess the utility of continued case count reporting. This research explored whether routine surveillance remains necessary in light of declining case volumes or shifting transmission risk.
Development of public health resources
Designed targeted knowledge translation tools for public health professionals in Canada, integrating the latest evidence on transmission, clinical manifestations, population-specific risks, and therapeutic options.




This work culminated in the development of a disease debrief published by the National Collaborating Centre for Infectious Diseases. The debrief serves as a practical, evidence-based guide for public health professionals and primary care physicians, offering up-to-date information on mpox case definitions, transmission trends, and emerging clade characteristics.


  Featured Resource




  
  
    
  

  
  

    Mpox Disease Debrief

    
      A knowledge translation resource for Canadian public health professionals, this debrief synthesizes current evidence on mpox epidemiology, emerging clades, and clinical guidance for frontline response and risk communication.
    

    
      
        View the Mpox Disease Debrief ›
      
    

  

\(~\)



This mpox research supports public health professionals in navigating a rapidly shifting outbreak landscape, ensuring that response efforts remain timely, evidence-informed, and tailored to the populations most at risk. The work also contributes to broader conversations on how national health systems can proactively adapt to emerging infectious diseases through applied epidemiology and actionable knowledge dissemination.
\(~\)
 This research is conducted in my capacity as a researcher with the National Collaborating Centre for Infectious Diseases (NCCID) at the Rady Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Manitoba. NCCID is one of six National Collaborating Centres for Public Health funded by the Public Health Agency of Canada, each focusing on a distinct area of public health, with the University of Manitoba serving as its host institution. This work reflects collaboration with academic, clinical, and public health partners to support timely, evidence-informed public health responses to mpox through accessible knowledge translation.

title: How I Found My Way to Public Health | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
How I Found My Way to Public Health
May 7, 2025
\(~\)
When I began studying molecular biology as an undergraduate, I came in with a very clear dream. I wanted to help cure cancer.
At the time, that felt like the most meaningful way I could use science. I thought that if I could understand disease at the level of cells, genes, and molecules, I could contribute, even in a small way, to one of the world’s biggest medical challenges.
What drew me in was the human body itself. Even when everything was working normally, there was so much to understand. Once disease entered the picture, it became even harder. I remember sitting with that complexity and realizing how difficult science really is. That realization made me respect the scientific process more, but it also made me question whether this was the kind of work I wanted to spend my life doing.
The more I learned, the more I understood that cancer is not one disease, and that many cancers remain difficult to prevent, treat, or cure despite decades of research and extraordinary progress. Science can be powerful and still painfully slow. I started to wonder whether I was on the right path.
Fortunately, I had the chance to step outside the lab and explore something new.
I began working as a research assistant at the Children’s Hospital Research Institute of Manitoba, contributing to studies in population health. That was my first real exposure to public health, and it changed how I thought about impact. I saw that public health was not only about studying disease. It was about changing the conditions that make people sick in the first place.
Then the COVID-19 pandemic arrived.
I was given the opportunity to work as an infectious disease epidemiologist for the Government of Manitoba. In that role, I worked alongside people across the public health response, from epidemiology and laboratory teams to medical officers of health. Decisions had to be made quickly. The work moved fast, and the stakes were real. For the first time, I felt like I had found where I belonged.
Infectious diseases brought together the parts of science I cared about most. Biology mattered, but it was never enough on its own. The work felt immediate. It also felt deeply human.
After my experience with COVID-19, my attention turned to another crisis. One that had been ongoing for decades, but still demanded far more action: HIV and sexually transmitted infections.
From my molecular biology background, I understood how formidable HIV is as a virus. Its error-prone reverse transcriptase allows it to mutate quickly, which is one reason a cure and a long-lasting vaccine have remained so difficult. But I also knew that HIV is not only a scientific challenge anymore. In many places, the harder problem is whether people can actually reach the help we already know works.
I began to think about my role differently. I may not be the person who cures HIV, but I can still contribute to preventing transmission, reducing harm, and making care more reachable for people who need it.
This work became even more personal when I reflected on where I came from. Growing up in a Filipino context, I saw how rarely sexual health was discussed openly. It was often treated as shameful, something to avoid rather than something people should be able to ask about safely.
In the Philippines, HIV incidence has been rising, especially among the youth. Despite progress in many parts of the world, stigma continues to stand between many people and the prevention or care they need.
But culture is only part of the story. Systems also shape who receives information, who feels safe seeking care, and who is left to struggle alone.
I think about people who avoid testing because they expect judgment, and communities that are blamed long before they are supported. I think about how poverty can narrow a person’s choices before public health ever reaches them.
When I was seven years old, I knew of children my age who were being sexually exploited. Not because they had real choices, but because they were trying to survive, help their families, or stay in school. I have never forgotten that.
I think about those children often. They deserved protection, safety, and a future. They still do.
At the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, I have found a way to bring my scientific training closer to the kind of public health work I care about. Through my work in HIV and STI, I want my research to be grounded in evidence without losing sight of the people that evidence is meant to serve.
My goal is not only to publish academic papers. Academic research matters, but it is not enough on its own. I also want to create work that people outside academia can use and understand. Sometimes that means writing. Sometimes it means teaching or making complex evidence easier to understand.
This site is one place where I share what I am learning, what I am working on, and what I am still trying to understand.
Most importantly, it’s a space to keep asking the questions that matter:

How do we reduce transmission – not just scientifically, but equitably?
How do we support people living with HIV – medically, emotionally, and socially?
How do we dismantle the stigma that continues to surround sexual health?

Public health is never neutral. It is shaped by who has power, whose suffering is noticed, and whose needs are ignored. That is what makes the work complicated. It is also what makes it necessary.
I don’t have all the answers. But I know I want to keep doing work that makes care more possible for the people who need it most.
-Ayel
\(~\)

title: Let’s Talk About STIs: What They Are, Why They Matter, and What You Can Do | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
Let’s Talk About STIs: What They Are, Why They Matter, and What You Can Do
May 10, 2025
\(~\)
Sexual health is a part of health. Full stop. Yet even in 2025, sexually transmitted infections (STIs) remain heavily stigmatized, misunderstood, and under-discussed. And because we don’t talk about them enough, we allow misinformation, fear, and shame to take the place of facts, care, and prevention.
This blog post is my small way of changing that – by starting with the basics, and by sharing some of my own perspective.

What Are STIs?
STIs – short for sexually transmitted infections – are infections that are primarily spread through sexual contact or exposure to infected blood. They can be passed through vaginal, anal, or oral sex, as well as through needle sharing, blood transfusions (where screening is limited), or from a birthing parent to child during pregnancy or childbirth.
Some of the most common STIs include:

Chlamydia
Gonorrhea
Syphilis
HPV (Human Papillomavirus)
Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV-1 and HSV-2)
HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus)
Hepatitis B and C

Each of these infections is different – in symptoms, transmission, and treatment – but they share one thing in common: they are all manageable, preventable, and in many cases, treatable.


How Common Are STIs?
Very common. More common than most people think. According to the World Health Organization, more than 1 million STIs are acquired every day worldwide. Many people living with STIs do not even know they have one, because symptoms are often mild or absent. This is why testing is so important.
The reality is: if you are sexually active, you could be at risk. Regardless of age, gender, relationship status, or sexual orientation.


Why Should We Care?
STIs do not always cause symptoms right away. But left untreated, they can lead to serious health consequences:

Infertility
Chronic pain
Liver disease and cancer (especially from hepatitis B and C)
Neurological complications
Increased risk of acquiring or transmitting HIV

For pregnant women, some STIs can lead to complications like miscarriage, stillbirth, or congenital infections passed on to the baby.
But here is the good news: Most STIs can be easily tested for, and many can be cured or effectively managed with treatment. Early detection truly makes all the difference.


A Personal Perspective: Living Between Two Worlds
As someone who has lived in both a high-income country like Canada and a lower-middle-income country like the Philippines, I have seen firsthand the contrast in how sexual health is treated, taught, and accessed.
In Canada, I was fortunate to receive comprehensive sexual education in high school. We were taught about family planning methods like condoms and birth control pills, how to access them, and even how to use them properly. I still remember the condom demonstration using a model. A moment filled with nervous laughter from students, but also one that left a lasting impression. That education, awkward or not, was useful – and lifesaving.
Canada’s public health system also provides access to free sexual health clinics, where youth, including young girls, can access contraceptives, STI testing, and counselling without stigma or barriers. We were taught how to test, why it matters to know our status, and how to protect ourselves through safer sex or abstinence.
In the Philippines, the contrast is stark. Sexual health remains a deeply taboo topic, especially in schools and within families. Comprehensive sexual education is rare, and public conversations around STIs are even rarer. Access to testing, treatment, and prevention tools like pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) or even condoms can be limited. Not just logistically, but culturally. The silence is loud, and the stigma can be overwhelming.
That contrast continues to fuel my commitment to public health. Because education should not be a luxury. Testing should not be a privilege. And silence should never be the barrier that keeps people from knowing their status and protecting themselves.


What’s Holding Us Back?
The biggest barriers are not scientific. They are social.
Many people delay or avoid testing not because they lack access, but because of fear. Fear of being judged. Fear of what others might think. Fear of being associated with something “dirty” or “irresponsible”.
That stigma can be even more intense in communities where sex is rarely discussed openly. I have seen this play out personally, especially in Southeast Asian and religious contexts. But staying silent only allows the problem to grow.
The truth is, STIs do NOT discriminate. They can affect anyone, regardless of how many partners you have had, how careful you are, or who you are. Risk is not about identity. It’s about biology and behaviour, and more importantly, about access to information and care.


What You Can Do: Take Care, Without Shame
Here is what we can all do to protect ourselves and each other:

Get tested regularly. If you are sexually active, routine testing is part of responsible healthcare – just like going to the dentist or getting a physical.
Use protection. Condoms and dental dams help prevent many STIs. They are not perfect, but they are effective.
Know your options. Vaccines exist for HPV and hepatitis B. HIV prevention tools like PrEP and PEP are life-changing for many.
Talk about it. Honest conversations with partners – and within our communities – can break down stigma and build trust.
Get informed. The more we understand, the less room there is for shame.



A Final Word: Knowledge Is Power, and Silence Is a Risk We Can’t Afford
Talking about STIs should not be controversial. It should be standard practice. These infections are common, treatable, and preventable. Yet every day, people are put at risk not because they lack protection, but because they lack information, access, and support.
I have seen how the silence around sexual health can cause real harm. I have lived in systems that empower people to make informed choices; and systems that shame them into silence. And I can tell you this: When we stay quiet, we do not keep people safe. We leave them behind.
But we can choose something different.
We can choose to create communities where talking about sexual health is a sign of care, not shame. We can choose to push for better education, stronger services, and a healthcare system that leaves no one out. We can choose to get tested, get informed, and speak up. Not just for ourselves, but for those who cannot yet do so safely.
Because knowledge is more than power. It’s protection. It’s freedom. And it’s a form of love.
Thank you for being here. Let’s keep talking.
-Ayel

title: Anaphylaxis Research | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
\(~\)
Anaphylaxis is an acute and potentially life-threatening systemic allergic reaction that requires rapid recognition and immediate treatment with intramuscular adrenaline (epinephrine). Despite the availability of clinical guidelines and increasing global awareness, the management of anaphylaxis remains inconsistent, particularly in low-resource settings. Several barriers contribute to this gap, including limited access to adrenaline, delays in diagnosis, and inadequate emergency response systems. These challenges continue to drive preventable morbidity and mortality worldwide.


There is growing recognition of global variation in how anaphylaxis is treated across different healthcare systems. While adrenaline is the recommended first-line treatment, evidence shows it remains under-utilized worldwide, with antihistamines and corticosteroids often used instead in emergency and acute care settings. This variation may be influenced by differences in clinical training, national guidelines, healthcare infrastructure, or cultural practices.
Our current research is synthesizing global evidence on:

International differences in anaphylaxis treatment and outcomes

Access to adrenaline, including adrenaline auto-injectors (AAIs) and ampoules

Alignment with clinical guidelines across healthcare systems

Barriers to timely care, including regulatory, cost, and training constraints

This research aims to characterize diverse treatment practices across global contexts. The findings are intended to inform policy recommendations that support more consistent, evidence-based emergency care for anaphylaxis worldwide.

  Featured Publication




  
  
    
      
    
  

  
  

    
    

    
    
      This protocol presents a scoping review investigating global variation in the emergency management of anaphylaxis. The forthcoming review will map differences in treatment practices and evaluate alignment with international guidelines, particularly regarding adrenaline use. The review will also examine contextual factors driving variation and identify evidence gaps to inform future research and health system improvements. The findings will provide a structured overview of current practice and areas requiring further investigation.
    
    

  
    Read article
  


    
    
      
        Batac ALR, Wong-Pack A, Lê ML, Reyes JCR, Bhamra MK, Hong B, Feldman LY, Fong AT, Bégin P. Global Variation in Anaphylaxis Treatment: A Scoping Review Protocol. Clin Exp Allergy. 2026 May;56(5):556-558. doi: 10.1111/cea.70273. Epub 2026 Mar 6. PMID: 41792974.
      
    

  

\(~\)



In this work, I examined the introduction and integration of adrenaline auto-injectors (AAIs) in mainland China, highlighting several persistent challenges in a context where access had historically been limited:

Outlined the evolving landscape of anaphylaxis management in China

Highlighted systemic barriers to AAI access and implementation

Identified gaps in clinical training and public awareness

Explored how global best practices may inform national protocols

Contributed to broader discussions on health equity and emergency preparedness

This work underscores the need for sustained policy attention, enhanced clinical training, and system-level adaptation to expand access to life-saving anaphylaxis treatment and strengthen emergency preparedness.

  Featured Publication




  
  
    
      
    
  

  
  

    
    

    
    
      This publication explores anaphylaxis treatment in mainland China, focusing on the introduction of adrenaline auto-injectors. It highlights key challenges such as regulatory barriers, cost, and limited awareness, and proposes policy solutions to improve access to life-saving care within national emergency response systems.
    
    

  
    Read article
  


    
    
      
        Batac ALR. From Ampoules to Auto-Injectors: Advancing Anaphylaxis Management in China. Clin Exp Allergy. 2025 Aug;55(8):600-602. doi: 10.1111/cea.70028. Epub 2025 Mar 12. PMID: 40071384.
      
    

  

\(~\)



Anaphylaxis is both a clinical emergency and a public health concern. Our research addresses critical gaps in how anaphylaxis is recognized, treated, and prevented across diverse health systems. By examining variations in treatment practices and barriers to adrenaline access, this work informs strategies to reduce preventable morbidity and mortality on a population level.
Key public health contributions include:

Generating evidence to support policy and regulatory reform on adrenaline access

Informing training and education strategies for clinicians, emergency responders, and the public

Identifying opportunities to standardize emergency care protocols in alignment with global best practices

Contributing to health equity initiatives by addressing disparities in access to life-saving treatment

Supporting international collaboration on anaphylaxis research and response planning

This work aims to bridge clinical insight with system-level action, reinforcing the importance of coordinated, evidence-based approaches to allergy emergencies in public health planning and preparedness.
\(~\)
Stay tuned – publications and data visualizations related to this work will be made available here soon.
\(~\)
 This research is conducted under my leadership in collaboration with physician and allied health partners in Canada, the United Kingdom, and Australia. It reflects a shared commitment to advancing evidence-based and patient-centred approaches to the prevention, recognition, and management of anaphylaxis.

title: U = U: The Science, the Message, and Why It Matters | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
U = U: The Science, the Message, and Why It Matters
May 23, 2025
\(~\)
What if one of the most powerful tools we have to stop HIV transmission is something most people have never even heard of?
It’s called U = U. Short for Undetectable = Untransmittable. And it means that a person living with HIV who is on effective treatment and maintains an undetectable viral load cannot pass the virus to others through sex.
Not “reduced risk”.
Not “less likely”.
Zero transmission.
This isn’t just a message of hope. It’s a message of science. And it has the power to change how we think about HIV, how we support those who live with it, and how we end stigma once and for all.

What Does U = U Actually Mean?
When someone living with HIV takes antiretroviral therapy (ART) consistently, the amount of virus in their body drops to such low levels that it becomes undetectable on standard blood tests. The virus isn’t cured, but it is controlled.
And here’s the most important part: when HIV is undetectable, it cannot be passed on sexually.
This is not a theory. It’s a fact, confirmed by some of the most significant research studies in HIV science:

🧪 HPTN 052 Study (2011)
This groundbreaking clinical trial followed over 1,700 mostly heterosexual couples across multiple continents. One partner in each couple was HIV-positive. The study compared couples where the HIV-positive partner started treatment early with those who delayed treatment.
Only one linked transmission occurred in the early treatment group, and it happened shortly after therapy began. In contrast, 27 linked transmissions occurred in the delayed treatment group. This resulted in a 96% reduction in risk – one of the first and strongest proofs that ART can prevent transmission.

Read the HPTN 052 Study here



🧪 PARTNER Study (2016)
This European study followed 888 couples (340 gay male and 548 heterosexual) where one partner was HIV-positive and on effective ART. Over 1,238 couple-years of follow-up, participants reported tens of thousands of condomless sex acts.
No HIV transmissions occurred between partners. Of the 11 infections observed, genetic testing confirmed all came from outside the primary relationship. The takeaway? When someone is on ART and virally suppressed, they don’t transmit HIV. Even without condoms.

Read the PARTNER Study here



🧪 PARTNER2 Study (2019)
This follow-up study focused exclusively on gay male couples – a group often considered at higher risk for HIV transmission. Over 1,593 couple-years of follow-up, participants reported about 76,088 acts of condomless anal sex while the HIV-positive partner had a suppressed viral load.
Again, no HIV transmissions occurred. This study confirmed what PARTNER1 had shown and extended it to even the highest-risk types of sex: U = U holds true, even during anal sex among MSM partners.
These three landmark studies give us what is rare in public health: definitive evidence. People living with HIV who are undetectable cannot transmit the virus through sex. Full stop.

Read the PARTNER2 Study here




Why This Message Matters
Imagine being in a relationship, taking your medication every day, and knowing, with confidence, that you cannot transmit HIV to the person you love. That’s the kind of peace and freedom U = U makes possible.
But for too long, HIV has been surrounded by fear, misunderstanding, and silence. Many people still hold outdated ideas – that HIV can be passed through hugging, sharing food, or being near someone living with the virus. These misconceptions are not just wrong; they isolate people and fuel stigma.
U = U tells a different story. A story based in science. It reminds us that with access to treatment and care, people living with HIV can enjoy full, loving, and healthy lives. They can be intimate, have families, and move through the world without fear of harming others, because they can’t transmit the virus when undetectable.
This knowledge is not only medically important. It’s emotionally and socially liberating.


From Science to Society: Where We Stand Now
Despite the overwhelming evidence, U = U is still not widely communicated. In many parts of the world, healthcare workers may not mention it, patients may not be aware of it, and systems may not support the testing and treatment access needed to achieve undetectable status.
Stigma continues to be one of the biggest obstacles. Too many people living with HIV are still judged unfairly, discriminated against, or made to feel unsafe, even when they pose no risk to others.
This is why sharing U = U matters so deeply. It challenges fear with facts. It replaces shame with dignity. And it builds a foundation of trust – between people, partners, and health systems.


U = U and the Bigger Picture of HIV Prevention
U = U is a major breakthrough, but it’s part of a broader strategy for HIV prevention. Other essential tools include:

Routine HIV testing
PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) for HIV-negative individuals
Condoms and harm reduction programs
Supportive, stigma-free care environments

When these tools work together, they help create communities where HIV is no longer something to fear, but something we know how to manage, together.


Equity and Access Still Matter
It’s important to acknowledge that not everyone has equal access to ART, viral load monitoring, or supportive healthcare. In many low- and middle-income countries, and even in marginalized communities within high-income ones, systemic barriers still limit what’s possible.
That’s why U = U must go hand-in-hand with efforts to ensure universal access to treatment, routine testing, and public education. This message is only as powerful as it is reachable.
Everyone deserves the opportunity to be undetectable, and untransmittable.


Featuring the Message: U = U in One Glance
To support the points discussed above, the following infographic provides a clear and accessible summary of what U = U means and why it matters. It’s a helpful visual to reinforce key information and support understanding:

This image highlights several core takeaways:

What “undetectable” means: With effective HIV treatment, the virus is suppressed so low it can’t be detected by standard tests. This is called having an undetectable viral load.
What it means for prevention: A person with an undetectable viral load cannot pass HIV through sex. This is not just reassuring. It’s scientifically proven.



Why This Changes Everything
So what does this all mean for us, right now?
This message isn’t just about reducing risk. It’s about changing lives:

✅ U = U reduces stigma by replacing fear with facts. When people know that those with undetectable HIV cannot transmit the virus, it chips away at the misinformation that fuels rejection and discrimination.
✅ It encourages treatment and testing by showing that knowing your status and accessing ART has both personal and public benefits.
✅ It builds trust in public health by demonstrating how scientific evidence can empower people, not just diagnose them.
✅ It empowers people living with HIV to live openly, form relationships, and plan families. All without fear of transmission.

But while U = U addresses HIV prevention, it’s also important to remember:

🛡️ PrEP protects only against HIV – not other STIs.

PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) is a powerful tool that helps HIV-negative individuals stay HIV-negative. But it does not prevent other sexually transmitted infections. That means protection like condoms still plays a key role – especially when it comes to preventing infections like gonorrhea, chlamydia, syphilis, and hepatitis B or C.

⚠️ U = U doesn’t prevent other STIs.

Sexually transmitted infections like chlamydia, gonorrhea, syphilis, hepatitis B and C, and others can still be transmitted, even when HIV is undetectable. That’s why using condoms consistently and correctly remains essential. Condoms offer strong protection not only against HIV but also against a wide range of STIs.
By combining strategies like U = U, PrEP, and especially consistent condom use, individuals can build a well-rounded approach to sexual health that protects not only against HIV, but also against a wide range of other STIs – fostering both physical health and human dignity.
Being undetectable changes what’s possible. But a comprehensive approach to sexual health is what keeps everyone safe, healthy, and respected.


The Bottom Line
Let’s be absolutely clear:

A person living with HIV who is on effective treatment and has an undetectable viral load cannot transmit the virus sexually.
This truth is backed by science, supported by real-world evidence, and endorsed by leading global health organizations.
U = U is a public health breakthrough, and a message of hope, equity, and human dignity.

The more we share this message, the more we break down stigma, build trust, and empower people, no matter their status.
Thank you for being here. Let’s keep talking.
-Ayel

title: Research | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
My research centres on the social epidemiology of sexually transmitted infections (STI), with a focus on marginalized and underserved populations, including men who have sex with men (MSM) and sex worker communities. I am particularly interested in STI prevention and control, with an emphasis on the structural, behavioural, and social determinants that shape health inequities.
I also examine infectious disease prevention and response in low- and middle-income countries (LMIC) and low-resource settings. My current work includes evaluating pay-it-forward approaches as innovative behavioural interventions to improve access to gonorrhea and chlamydia testing among MSM in China. I also maintain broader interests in respiratory infections, including coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), influenza (flu), respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), and tuberculosis (TB). While I have growing interests in health economic and infectious disease modelling, my current focus lies in mixed methods research, knowledge synthesis, and epidemiological analysis. I am actively engaged in knowledge translation (KT) and knowledge mobilization (KMb) efforts to bridge research with policy and practice, ensuring that evidence informs real-world decision-making.
In addition to my work in infectious diseases, I have a longstanding interest in allergy and clinical immunology, particularly in the areas of anaphylaxis and allergen immunotherapy. I am also interested in the pharmacoeconomic evaluation of HIV prevention strategies, including pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP).

title: Syphilis Self-Testing: Access Is the Intervention | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
Syphilis Self-Testing: Access Is the Intervention
January 2, 2026

 TL;DR 


Syphilis is curable, yet cases continue to rise.


The problem is not the lack of treatment. It’s whether people can get tested early enough.


Centralized diagnostic systems work well for many people, but they can still miss those who face the greatest barriers to care.


Decentralized approaches, including syphilis self-testing, have helped expand access in many settings.


Self-testing does not replace healthcare. It works best when people know what to do next and can actually get care afterward.



Syphilis is curable. That is exactly why rising cases should unsettle us.
When a curable infection continues to spread, we have to look beyond the medicine itself. The harder question is whether testing systems are actually reaching the people who need them.
I think about this often because I have seen how differently testing and care work in the Philippines and Canada. Living first in the Philippines and later in Canada taught me that a service can exist on paper and still be difficult to reach in real life. I noticed that long before I knew how to describe it as a public health problem.
In the Philippines, I saw how much care could depend on what was nearby and what people could afford. In Canada and the United States, STI testing is usually routed through more formal clinic and laboratory systems. Those systems can be strong, but they also shape whether testing happens early enough and whether people can return for care.
That perspective shaped a recent invited commentary I co-authored in the Journal of Clinical Microbiology with Drs. Michael Marks, Joseph Tucker, and Rosanna Peeling, whose work has influenced how many of us think about diagnostics and self-testing.
In the commentary, we focused on a simple but difficult question:

Who is being missed by the way we currently test for syphilis?


When testing depends on the clinic
In Canada, STI testing usually means entering the formal healthcare system. A person gets tested through a clinic or hospital, the sample goes to a laboratory, and treatment depends on the result getting back to the right place at the right time.
This model has real strengths. It gives clinicians reliable results and creates a clear pathway for follow-up care. For many people, it works well.
But it also assumes that people can get to care and feel safe using it. That is not always true.
For someone who lives far from services, expects judgment, or has had bad experiences with healthcare, clinic-based testing may be technically available but practically out of reach. A system can be strong on paper and still miss the people who need it most.
That is why rising syphilis rates are not only about treatment. They are also about how testing is organized.


What decentralized testing can teach us
In many parts of Asia and Africa, decentralized testing did not emerge simply because it was convenient. It emerged because the usual clinic-and-lab model was not enough.
Rapid tests can be used outside large laboratories and give results quickly. That matters when travel is difficult, when people may not return for follow-up, or when stigma makes clinic visits feel unsafe.
There is an important trade-off. Rapid syphilis tests are useful for screening, but reactive results still need confirmatory testing. That does not mean the standard is lower. It means the testing strategy is being shaped around the reality of who can be reached.
I see this clearly through my work in China.
In one randomized trial among men who have sex with men, distributing dual HIV and syphilis self-testing kits through social networks increased testing compared with site-based testing. People with reactive results still accessed confirmatory testing, and the study did not find evidence of social harm.
Evidence from Zimbabwe points in a similar direction. In a study comparing facility-based syphilis testing with community-based self-testing among gay, bisexual, and transgender men, self-testing increased uptake and lowered the cost per test for users.
These studies do not mean self-testing solves everything. They show something more practical: testing reaches more people when it is easier to use.
For me, that is the central lesson. The design of a testing system affects who gets diagnosed and who gets care.


Where syphilis self-testing fits
Syphilis self-testing allows a person to test outside a clinic, usually with a finger-prick blood sample, and receive a result within minutes.
In 2024, the first at-home, over-the-counter syphilis self-test was authorized in the United States. That matters because high-income countries have long relied on centralized laboratory testing for STI diagnosis.
But a self-test is not the same as a diagnosis. Current syphilis self-tests detect treponemal antibodies, which can remain positive even after a past infection has been treated. A reactive result still needs confirmatory laboratory testing and clinical follow-up.
That point is important. Self-testing should not leave people on their own.
Used well, it can be a first step into care for people who might not walk into a clinic. Used poorly, it can create confusion, anxiety, or a result with nowhere to go.
Experience with HIV self-testing offers a useful lesson. The test itself is only one part of the intervention. What matters is whether people know what to do next and can actually get care afterward.
Syphilis self-testing has to follow the same principle.


Designing testing around real life
Self-testing can still leave people behind if we are not careful.
If tests are only sold online or in pharmacies, they may never reach the people who face the biggest barriers to clinic-based testing. If a person gets a reactive result but has no affordable way to confirm it or receive treatment, the system has still failed them.
The answer is not to replace laboratories. It is to make testing easier to reach while keeping strong pathways into care.
That means thinking beyond the test kit: how people will get it, what happens after a reactive result, and whether confirmatory testing feels reachable.
Those questions matter because syphilis is not difficult to treat. The harder problem is often getting people tested and connected to care early enough.


The bigger question
Health systems often work best for people who can move through them easily. But many people do not experience care that way.
People do not avoid STI testing for one reason. Sometimes it is shame. Sometimes it is work, distance, or not having a regular provider. Sometimes it is because healthcare has not felt safe before.
A diagnostic system that only works for people who can comfortably enter the clinic will always miss someone.
That is why syphilis self-testing is worth taking seriously. Not because it is perfect, and not because it can replace clinical care. It matters because it asks a different question: what would testing look like if we designed it around the people least likely to be reached by the current system?


The bottom line
Syphilis self-testing will not solve rising syphilis rates on its own.
But it can help reach people who are not being reached by clinic-based testing alone. It can make testing feel more private and less dependent on whether someone is willing or able to enter the healthcare system first.
The promise is not the test by itself. The promise is what becomes possible when testing is easier to reach and care is ready to respond.
-Ayel
\(~\)

\(~\)

Read the Commentary
The full commentary is available in the Journal of Clinical Microbiology (Editor’s Pick).

Read the full commentary here


Commentary Authors
This commentary was written by a team of researchers whose work spans diagnostics, infectious diseases, and global health, with a shared focus on access, equity, and real-world impact.
Ayel L. R. Batac is a public health researcher at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, and a researcher with the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and SESH Global, the China hub of the Social Innovation in Health Initiative (TDR, the Special Programme for Research and Training in Tropical Diseases). His research focuses on the social epidemiology of sexually transmitted infections, with an emphasis on inequities in access to testing and prevention among marginalized populations.
Dr. Michael Marks is a Professor of Medicine at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine and an infectious diseases consultant at the Hospital for Tropical Diseases, University College London Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust. His research focuses on the epidemiology, diagnosis, and control of infectious diseases, including syphilis and other neglected tropical diseases, with particular emphasis on community-based treatment strategies and integrated approaches to disease control.
Dr. Joseph Tucker is a Professor of Global Health at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine and a Professor of Medicine at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. His work centers on the prevention and control of sexually transmitted infections, including syphilis, and on the use of crowdsourcing, social networks, and community-driven approaches to expand access to testing and care, particularly in global and low-resource settings.
Dr. Rosanna Peeling is an Emeritus Professor of Diagnostics Research at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine and a global leader in diagnostic innovation. Trained as a medical microbiologist, her work has been foundational in shaping how diagnostics for HIV, syphilis, and other infectious diseases are developed, evaluated, and implemented across diverse global contexts.
Reference: Batac ALR, Marks M, Tucker JD, Peeling RW. Syphilis self-testing and implications for syphilis control and prevention. J Clin Microbiol. 2025 Dec 17;63(12):e0098225. doi: 10.1128/jcm.00982-25. Epub 2025 Oct 21. PMID: 41117600; PMCID: PMC12710337.

title: Tuberculosis Research | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
\(~\)
Tuberculosis (TB) remains a major public health concern in remote Arctic communities across Canada, where incidence rates are consistently the highest in the country. Despite long-standing efforts to control and eliminate the disease, TB continues to re-emerge in the form of outbreaks, particularly in geographically isolated settings where social and structural challenges persist.
In these contexts, barriers such as limited access to healthcare, overcrowded housing, and long-standing distrust in public health systems undermine prevention and control efforts. These challenges are often compounded by logistical obstacles such as seasonal mobility, infrastructure limitations, and staff turnover, that constrain timely diagnosis and treatment.


One of the most promising interventions to disrupt TB transmission in high-burden regions is community-wide screening. This approach involves proactively offering screening to all members of a community, regardless of symptoms, to detect both latent TB infection (LTBI) and active TB disease.
The benefits of community-wide screening include:

Reducing time to diagnosis, thereby interrupting transmission chains

Identifying latent infections before they progress to active disease

Providing culturally adapted outreach and education to increase participation

Strengthening community trust through local leadership and engagement

Previous implementations of community-wide screening in Nunavut, Canada, have demonstrated both effectiveness and cost-efficiency when adapted to local realities. When combined with targeted health promotion and community-driven planning, these initiatives support early detection, treatment uptake, and long-term disease control.



My current work focuses on the evaluation of a community-wide screening clinic conducted in response to a TB outbreak in Nunavut. This evaluation examines how the initiative:

Detected and treated both active and latent TB cases

Promoted awareness and reduced stigma through bilingual outreach

Addressed barriers to participation and treatment completion

Applied lessons from previous community-wide screening efforts to strengthen delivery

This research contributes to operational knowledge on how community-based strategies can strengthen TB prevention and control in remote, high-incidence regions.



This evaluation takes a quantitative, epidemiological approach to assess the outcomes of a community-wide screening initiative conducted in response to a TB outbreak in Nunavut. Program data were analyzed to examine screening coverage, diagnostic outcomes, and treatment initiation for both active TB and LTBI.
The study also highlights the critical role of community engagement and local leadership in the success of the community-wide screening. The planning and implementation process incorporated culturally grounded strategies to promote trust, reduce stigma, and enhance participation. Clinic delivery was shaped by coordinated interagency efforts and informed by previous screening initiatives in the territory.
By combining epidemiological findings with high-level insights into operational planning and community engagement, this evaluation provides practical evidence to guide future TB control efforts in remote and high-incidence settings, while respecting the local context and the principles of cultural safety.



This research advances understanding of how community-wide screening can serve as an effective and context-sensitive strategy for TB control in remote Arctic communities. Key contributions include:

Providing real-world evidence on the epidemiological impact of community-wide screening in a high-incidence, underserved setting

Highlighting the role of community engagement in increasing screening uptake and reducing barriers to participation

Demonstrating how operational planning, when informed by local knowledge and interagency collaboration, can enhance intervention effectiveness

Informing future TB response strategies that centre cultural safety, community leadership, and adaptability to local realities

By emphasizing trust, collaboration, and responsiveness to community needs, this work supports the broader vision of TB elimination in Nunavut and contributes to national public health goals rooted in equity and reconciliation.
\(~\)
Stay tuned – publications and data visualizations related to this work will be made available here soon.
\(~\)
 This research is conducted in my capacity as a researcher with the National Collaborating Centre for Infectious Diseases (NCCID), in collaboration with the Government of Nunavut’s Office of the Chief Public Health Officer and Department of Health, and reflects a shared commitment to advancing community-led and culturally grounded approaches to tuberculosis prevention and control in northern Canada.

Informing future TB response strategies that centre cultural safety, community leadership, and adaptability to local realities

By emphasizing trust, collaboration, and responsiveness to community needs, this work supports the broader vision of TB elimination in Nunavut and contributes to national public health goals rooted in equity and reconciliation.
\(~\)
Stay tuned – publications and data visualizations related to this work will be made available here soon.
\(~\)
 This research is conducted in my capacity as a researcher with the National Collaborating Centre for Infectious Diseases (NCCID), in collaboration with the Government of Nunavut’s Office of the Chief Public Health Officer and Department of Health, and reflects a shared commitment to advancing community-led and culturally grounded approaches to tuberculosis prevention and control in northern Canada.

title: Guidelines Are Global. Access Is Not: Structural Gaps in Anaphylaxis Treatment | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
Guidelines Are Global. Access Is Not: Structural Gaps in Anaphylaxis Treatment
March 10, 2026

 TL;DR 


Anaphylaxis is life-threatening and requires immediate treatment with epinephrine.


International guidelines are clear, but access to recommended treatment still varies across health systems.


New options like intranasal epinephrine may make emergency treatment easier for some people.


But a treatment only protects people if they can actually reach it when they need it.



Epinephrine, also known as adrenaline, is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. That part is clear.
The harder question is whether people can actually get it when they need it.
That question has stayed with me because I have seen how differently medications are accessed in the Philippines and Canada. In the Philippines, I was used to seeing nebulizers in homes and clinics. A nebulizer machine could be bought once, and salbutamol solution was familiar and relatively inexpensive. For many families, that made sense.
Metered-dose inhalers, or “puffers” felt different. They were portable and commonly used in places like Canada, but they also required refills and could feel more expensive over time. In Canada, I saw the opposite pattern: inhalers were routine, while nebulizers were usually reserved for more specific clinical situations.
Neither approach is random. People adapt to the system they are given.
That experience changed how I think about medications. A treatment can be clinically standard and still feel out of reach. What families keep at home is shaped long before an emergency happens.
That is the lens I bring to anaphylaxis.
Anaphylaxis is one of the most time-sensitive emergencies in medicine. Across major allergy guidelines, the recommendation is consistent: epinephrine should be given promptly by intramuscular injection.
But a clear recommendation does not mean practice looks the same everywhere.
That perspective shaped an editorial I wrote on the introduction of epinephrine auto-injectors in mainland China. It also continues to inform my collaborative work with physician and allied health partners across Canada and Australia.
The question I keep coming back to is simple:

If epinephrine is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis, why does treatment still look so different in practice?

That question led us to examine how anaphylaxis is managed across health systems and to develop a scoping review protocol to guide that work.

What medication access looks like in real life
Guidelines often make treatment sound straightforward. Real emergencies are shaped by what the system has made reachable before the reaction begins.
In many parts of Asia, including the Philippines, epinephrine auto-injectors are not routinely stocked in retail pharmacies. Families may instead rely on ampoules, which require someone to prepare and give the medication correctly under pressure. In other places, the product may exist but still be hard to obtain because of price, supply, or regulation.
This changes what preparedness means. It’s one thing to know that epinephrine is first-line treatment. It’s another thing to have a device nearby and feel prepared to use it.
I explored this issue in Clinical & Experimental Allergy through the introduction of epinephrine auto-injectors in mainland China. In 2023, they were approved for clinical use in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area, but broader availability across mainland China remains limited.
An allergic reaction does not change based on geography, but access to first-line treatment often does.


Why practice does not always follow guidelines
It can be tempting to describe differences in anaphylaxis treatment as “non-adherence” to guidelines. Sometimes that may be true. But often, the situation is more complicated.
By the time an emergency happens, the system has already narrowed the options. A clinician can only reach for what has been approved, supplied, and taught. A family can only use what they were able to obtain before the emergency began.
In some settings, evidence suggests that epinephrine is not always given first. Antihistamines or corticosteroids may be used before epinephrine, treatment may be delayed, or a different route may be used. These differences matter because anaphylaxis moves quickly, and delayed treatment can change the course of an emergency.
The allergic reaction begins in the body, but what happens next depends on what the health system has made possible.
That is why new treatment options matter.


A changing landscape: intranasal epinephrine
The treatment landscape for anaphylaxis is beginning to change.
neffy®, a needle-free nasal epinephrine spray, has been approved in the United States and Canada for emergency treatment of severe allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis. For some people, a nasal spray may feel less intimidating than an injection. That matters in an emergency, where hesitation can cost time.
Emerging evidence suggests that intranasal epinephrine can produce epinephrine levels in the body that are comparable to intramuscular delivery in appropriate contexts. Some recent guidance has also begun to discuss intranasal epinephrine as an alternative option in selected clinical situations.
This is real progress. But approval is only the first step.
A new product does not automatically make care more equal. After approval, the practical question is whether the product reaches the people for whom an injection is a barrier. If it remains expensive or concentrated in well-resourced markets, it could widen the very gaps it has the potential to narrow.
The question is not only whether innovation expands what is possible. It is who gets to benefit from it.


Why this work matters
Much of the anaphylaxis literature focuses on how often anaphylaxis happens and what causes it. Those questions matter.
I am also interested in what happens after a guideline is written. Does epinephrine actually become the first treatment people receive, or does practice change depending on where the emergency happens?
These are not abstract questions. They are the difference between a treatment that exists in a guideline and a treatment that is actually reachable during an emergency.
Mapping global variation is not about ranking countries. It’s about understanding why treatment becomes possible in one setting and difficult in another.
When a life-saving treatment exists but people cannot reliably reach it, the problem is bigger than clinical variation. It means the system is deciding, quietly and unevenly, who has a better chance in an emergency.


The bigger question
Emergency care is often assumed to be standardized, especially when the guideline is clear. But emergencies do not happen outside inequality.
Long before an allergic reaction occurs, the system has already shaped the response. It has already shaped what is within reach.
Guidelines matter. But they do not treat anyone unless people can act on them.
No child’s survival should depend on where they were born.


The bottom line
Anaphylaxis is life-threatening. We know what first-line treatment should be. New options, including intranasal epinephrine, are expanding what may be possible.
But consensus alone is not enough.
Until first-line treatment is reachable when and where people need it, guidelines alone will not save lives.
-Ayel

title: About | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
ABOUT
\(~\)





   
   
   
   
   




Ayel Luis R. Batac
Public Health Scientist and Health Systems Researcher
Ayel holds the following positions:

Scientific Researcher, Faculty of Public Health and Policy, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine

Member, Centre for Epidemic Preparedness and Response, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine

Member, Centre for Data and Statistical Science for Health, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine

Member, Centre for Maternal, Adolescent, Reproductive, and Child Health, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine
Member, Centre for Evaluation, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine

Researcher, Social Engagement to Spur Health (SESH Global), China hub of Social Innovation in Health Initiative, TDR, the Special Programme for Research and Training in Tropical Diseases

Researcher, Institute for Global Health and Infectious Diseases, School of Medicine, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

Research Affiliate, Dermatology Hospital of Southern Medical University (南方医科大学皮肤病医院), Guangdong Provincial Dermatology Hospital (广东省皮肤病医院)

Researcher, National Collaborating Centre for Infectious Diseases, Rady Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Manitoba







I am an epidemiologist, health economist, and implementation scientist whose work examines how evidence can be used to improve access, strengthen service delivery, and support more equitable public health decisions. I am currently pursuing a Master of Science in Public Health with a concentration in Health Economics at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, and I bring over six years of experience in research, data analytics, and applied public health practice. My current research asks how health systems can improve access to prevention and care for populations facing heightened health risks. This includes evaluating behavioural interventions to increase sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing among men who have sex with men (MSM) in China, leading research on global access to anaphylaxis treatment and emergency care preparedness, and assessing tuberculosis screening strategies in remote Arctic communities in Canada.
My earlier research was situated at the intersection of allergy and clinical immunology, pediatrics and child health, infectious disease prevention, and applied public health. Within this work, I examined COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy and vaccine-associated allergic reactions to support safe and inclusive immunization strategies. I also studied the impact of atopic dermatitis on maternal–infant bonding, reflecting my interest in the psychosocial dimensions of chronic disease and the ways health systems shape patient and family experiences. This research has been complemented by applied public health practice in infectious disease epidemiology, grounded in surveillance and outbreak response. I approach mixed methods not only as a research strategy, but as a way to understand public health problems in their full complexity, including the systems, behaviours, and contexts that shape them.
A central aim of my research is to produce work that is useful beyond academic settings. Rather than asking only whether an intervention or policy works, I am interested in how it works, for whom, and under what conditions. My work has appeared in leading journals and has been shaped by collaborations spanning North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia. Across these collaborations and areas of practice, I remain committed to research that is grounded in strong methods, responsive to real-world public health needs, and attentive to populations with the greatest need.

title: Sexually Transmitted Infections Research | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
\(~\)
Sexually transmitted infections (STIs), including gonorrhoea and chlamydia, remain a major public health concern worldwide. Asymptomatic infections frequently go undiagnosed, delaying treatment and hindering effective prevention and control. Timely testing is therefore essential, yet testing coverage remains low in many settings, especially among men who have sex with men (MSM).
MSM are disproportionately affected by STIs, but many face barriers to testing and care. In many low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), stigma, concerns about confidentiality, and out-of-pocket costs can limit access to STI services, even when testing is available. Structural exclusion and social marginalization can further reduce engagement with formal health systems, widening gaps in access to timely testing and care.


The pay-it-forward strategy is a social innovation that connects public health service delivery with prosocial behaviour. In the context of STI prevention and testing, individuals are offered free diagnostic services, such as testing for gonorrhoea and chlamydia, and are then invited to make a voluntary contribution to support testing for someone else.
The approach works by reducing the immediate cost of testing while creating space for people to support others who may face similar barriers.

Access: reducing financial barriers to testing

Altruism: encouraging reciprocity and voluntary support for others

Community engagement: strengthening shared responsibility for health and well-being

Unlike traditional fee-for-service models, the pay-it-forward approach creates opportunities for community support within the delivery of health services. It offers a practical model for improving access to essential services in settings where stigma, financial constraints, or mistrust of institutions can limit care.



The pay-it-forward approach represents more than a mechanism for service delivery—it redefines how communities participate in public health. When combined with community engagement, it creates enabling environments where testing becomes accessible, supportive, and responsive to stigma.
Community engagement activities, including peer outreach and local advocacy, help strengthen collective ownership of sexual health and reinforce trust between health systems and communities. Together, these efforts:

Empower individuals to take part in collective health promotion

Foster trust and cooperation between communities and health services

Expand the reach and uptake of STI testing, particularly in LMIC settings




My current research evaluates the effectiveness and implementation of the pay-it-forward approach among MSM in China through a randomized controlled trial (RCT). Key areas of focus include:

Uptake of STI testing across different behavioural intervention arms

Prosocial behaviours as indicators of community engagement and reciprocity

Clinic-level and demographic determinants of testing outcomes

Adaptation of a digital, self-sampling version of the pay-it-forward approach to enhance accessibility in low-resource contexts

This research contributes to the growing global evidence base on how social innovation and behavioural science can strengthen health systems, improve testing coverage, and address the needs of populations most affected by stigma and inequity.



The research applies a mixed methods design to capture both impact and implementation outcomes:

Quantitative analysis: Randomized controlled trials assess behavioural and clinical outcomes, including testing uptake and donation patterns.

Qualitative inquiry: In-depth interviews with participants and providers explore motivations, perceived barriers, and emotional responses.

Process evaluation: Implementation fidelity, feasibility, and scalability are assessed under real-world conditions.

This comprehensive approach supports both rigorous evidence generation and context-sensitive insights that inform adaptation and scale-up.



The pay-it-forward approach demonstrates how social innovation can advance equity, inclusion, and sustainability within public health systems. Evidence from ongoing research highlights its potential to:

Expand access to essential health services through community-driven and decentralized financing models

Promote stigma-sensitive and culturally responsive testing practices tailored to the experiences of MSM and other key populations

Strengthen the interface between health systems and communities by embedding social innovation within service delivery

Contribute to global efforts toward universal health coverage and equitable access to sexual and reproductive health services

By framing STI testing as a collective act of care, the pay-it-forward approach aligns with broader global health goals to build inclusive, resilient, and people-centred systems for sexual health and well-being.
\(~\)
Stay tuned – publications and data visualizations related to this work will be made available here soon.
\(~\)
 This research is conducted in my capacity as a researcher at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. It forms part of an investigator-led study led by Drs. Joseph Tucker and Weiming Tang and reflects collaboration with academic and clinical partners at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, the Dermatology Hospital of Southern Medical University, and SESH Global (China hub of the Social Innovation in Health Initiative, TDR, the Special Programme for Research and Training in Tropical Diseases). 

\(~\)

title: neffy in Canada: Same Epinephrine. New Delivery Option. | Ayel L. R. Batac
text:
neffy in Canada: Same Epinephrine. New Delivery Option.
May 15, 2026

 TL;DR 


neffy® is a 2 mg epinephrine nasal spray authorized in Canada for emergency treatment of allergic reactions in people who weigh 30 kg or more.


Epinephrine remains the first-line emergency treatment for anaphylaxis.


neffy does not prevent allergic reactions; after epinephrine is used, people should call 911 or contact local emergency services.


Drug-level and body-response studies support neffy as an emergency epinephrine delivery option, but real-world use during actual anaphylaxis events still needs to be studied.


A new delivery option only helps if people know when to use it, how to use it, and what to do afterward.



What changes when the medication is the same, but the way it’s delivered changes?
That is the question I keep coming back to as neffy becomes available in Canada.
neffy is still epinephrine. It’s still used for emergency treatment of severe allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis. The difference is how it’s given: instead of being injected into the thigh, it’s sprayed into one nostril.
Health Canada has authorized neffy for emergency treatment of allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis, in adults and children who weigh 30 kg or more. These reactions may be triggered by insect stings or bites, foods, medications, or other allergens. They may also be idiopathic or exercise-induced. Health Canada has not authorized neffy for patients under 30 kg.
But this is not just a story about a new product. It’s also about whether people are prepared to use it correctly when an emergency happens.

What neffy is, and what it is not
neffy is a needle-free epinephrine nasal spray for emergencies. It’s not an allergy cure. It doesn’t prevent allergic reactions. It doesn’t replace allergen avoidance, an anaphylaxis emergency plan, or the need to contact emergency services after epinephrine is used.
According to the Canadian product monograph, the recommended dose is one spray, containing 2 mg of epinephrine, into one nostril. If symptoms do not improve or symptoms worsen after the first dose, a second dose may be given in the same nostril using a new nasal spray, starting five minutes after the first dose. The product monograph also recommends that patients have immediate access to two neffy nasal sprays at all times.
A nasal spray may seem simple. Anaphylaxis is not.
In an emergency, symptoms can progress quickly. People may panic, hesitate, or second-guess what they are seeing. So even though neffy may look more familiar than an injectable device, it is still epinephrine for a potentially life-threatening situation.
For some people, neffy may be a welcome alternative to an auto-injector. For others, the best option will depend on their clinical history, preferences, access, and emergency plan.


Why the device matters
On paper, neffy is still epinephrine. In real life, the device matters.
A person may know they are supposed to use epinephrine and still hesitate. They may be afraid of needles. A parent or caregiver may worry about using an injectable device incorrectly. Someone nearby may not be sure whether the symptoms are “bad enough”. In the middle of an emergency, even a simple set of instructions can feel harder to follow.
That is one reason neffy is important. A needle-free option may feel less intimidating for some patients, caregivers, teachers, or bystanders. It may also be easier to explain in certain community settings.
But a different device also means different training. It’s not enough to ask whether someone “carries epinephrine”. We need to know which device they carry, where it’s kept, who knows how to use it, and who will call emergency services afterward.


What the evidence shows so far
The question is not whether epinephrine has a role in anaphylaxis. It does. Epinephrine is already the first-line emergency treatment.
The more specific question for neffy is whether a nasal spray can deliver epinephrine into the body in a way that supports emergency use.
Health Canada’s product monograph states that controlled clinical trials for the treatment of anaphylaxis have not been performed with epinephrine. It also states that after one 2 mg nasal dose of neffy, epinephrine levels in the body were generally within the range seen after an epinephrine injection into the muscle over 60 minutes.
Published neffy studies provide supportive evidence about how the medication moves through the body and affects the body after use. In a repeat-dosing study, neffy produced epinephrine levels and physiologic responses within a comparable range to intramuscular epinephrine, including in people with allergy-related nasal symptoms. The same study also supported repeat dosing in the same nostril.
These studies mainly tell us what happens to epinephrine levels in the body after neffy is used. They help answer whether the medication reaches the body. They don’t prove that neffy will improve real-world emergency outcomes compared with existing epinephrine devices. They also don’t tell us whether every person will carry it consistently, recognize anaphylaxis quickly, or use it correctly under stress.
Broader research on intranasal adrenaline supports the scientific plausibility of nasal delivery, but not all of that evidence is specific to neffy. A recent review concluded that clinical efficacy data remain limited, and that long-term real-world evidence on effectiveness and adherence in community settings is still needed.
Based on the evidence so far, neffy appears to be a reasonable emergency epinephrine option for eligible patients. But it still needs to be paired with training, clear instructions, and follow-up in real-world settings.


What Canadians should consider next
For Canadians considering neffy, there are three practical details to sort out.
First, check eligibility. neffy 2 mg is authorized in Canada for people who weigh 30 kg or more. The 1 mg dose for children under 30 kg is not available in Canada at this time.
Second, ask about cost and availability. Food Allergy Canada has reported that neffy is expected to be available in Canadian pharmacies starting in summer 2026. The price will depend on coverage and reimbursement arrangements with provinces and insurance companies.
Third, update the emergency plan around the actual device. If someone carries neffy, the plan should name neffy specifically. It should say that it’s a single-use nasal spray, that a second dose may be given in the same nostril with a new device after five minutes if symptoms do not improve or worsen, and that 911 or local emergency services should be called after epinephrine is used.
The device should be named in the plan, not just the medication.
Patients and families don’t need to make this decision alone. An allergist, primary care provider, or pharmacist can help determine whether neffy fits their allergy history, daily life, and emergency plan.
As neffy becomes available in Canada, access is only part of the story. What matters most is whether the people closest to an allergic emergency are ready to recognize it, use the device, and act without hesitation.
-Ayel
